name: Raspberry Currant Coulis (Rodegruett)
recipeIngredient: 2 1/2 cups Red currants, stems removed and washed
1 cup to 1 1/2 cups Sugar (depending on tartness of the berries)
5 tbsp to 6 tbsp Cornstarch dissolved in 3/4 cup cold water
recipeInstructions:
1. Cook the berries in water for 15 minutes until soft.
2. Press through a fine strainer.
3. Add sugar and vanilla from slit bean to fruit juice; bring to boil in non-reactive pot.
4. Reduce while stirring vigorously.
5. Add dissolved cornstarch to sweetened fruit juice; bring to boil once more.
6. Transfer Rodegruett to a glass baking dish rinsed with cold water.
7. Chill in refrigerator and serve.
Note:
Serve in individual bowls rather than one large serving dish. Serve with cream, cold milk, or Vanilla Sauce poured over groats before eating.
Ensure groats are not too thick; consistency should be between pudding and puree (like thick pea soup).
When first tasted, Rodegruett may seem too sweet or berry flavor overwhelming, but after cooling and adding milk or cream, taste milder.
Variations:
Replace strained berry pulp with equivalent fruit juice.
Use frozen berries or preserves instead of fresh ones.
Cherries, morellos, or black currants can replace red currants and raspberries.
Hold back some berries for later to avoid cooking down; still tasteable while eating.
Tapioca is often used as a binding agent; farina (semolina) may be the original method.
Fortify Rodegruett by stewing in red wine or higher-proof alcohol.
Makes 4 to 6 servings.
